rows.yaml:
collection: Compiler Demo
description: Smallest possible end-to-end — start, set a variable, call a connector.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: Hello Connector
    description: Demonstrates start -> set_variable -> connector flow.
    steps:
      - name: Start
        type: start
        next: Prepare inputs

      - name: Prepare inputs
        type: set_variable
        next: Get organization
        vars:
          target_org: "Fortinet"

      - name: Get organization
        type: connector
        arguments:
          connector: fortinet-fortisiem
          operation: get_org_name_by_org_id
          config: ""
          params:
            domain_id: "{{ vars.target_org }}"

# Branching: a Decision step routes to different next-steps based on
# a condition. Each entry under `conditions:` has a `when:` expression
# and `next:` target; one entry has `default: true` for the else path.

collection: Compiler Examples
description: Decision branching with high/low severity routing.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: Route By Severity
    description: Demonstrate decision branches.
    steps:
      - name: start
        type: start
        next: Read severity

      - name: Read severity
        type: set_variable
        next: Branch on severity
        vars:
          severity: "{{ vars.input.records[0].severity }}"

      - name: Branch on severity
        type: decision
        conditions:
          - display: high
            when: "{{ vars.severity in ['critical', 'high'] }}"
            next: Escalate to Tier 2
          - display: Else
            default: true
            next: Log low severity

      - name: Escalate to Tier 2
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          tier: "tier2"

      - name: Log low severity
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          tier: "tier1"

# Record-context trigger demo (cybersponse.action). Half of all
# real-world FSR playbooks fire this way — right-click an alert,
# pick "Execute". The playbook receives the record at vars.input.records[0]
# and stamps a verdict.
#
# `type: record_action` compiles to cybersponse.action with the canonical
# dozen-key argument dict (route uuid, resources, displayConditions,
# triggerOnSource, etc.) auto-filled.

collection: FSRPB Demo Alert Action
description: Right-click action on alerts — read severity, decide, stamp.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB Demo - Triage Alert
    description: Reads alert severity and tags the alert with a verdict.
    is_active: true
    steps:
      - name: Triage Alert            # canvas/step name (what shows on the node)
        type: start
        next: Read severity
        arguments:
          module: alerts
          button_label: "Triage Alert" # what shows on the Execute menu button
          requires_record: true
          run_mode: per_record

      - name: Read severity
        type: set_variable
        next: Branch on severity
        vars:
          severity: "{{ vars.input.records[0].severity.itemValue }}"

      - name: Branch on severity
        type: decision
        conditions:
          - display: high
            when: "{{ vars.severity in ['Critical', 'High'] }}"
            next: Stamp high verdict
          - display: low
            when: "{{ vars.severity not in ['Critical', 'High'] }}"
            next: No action low severity

      - name: Stamp high verdict
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          verdict: "escalate"

      - name: No action low severity
        type: end

warnings.message: decision 'branch_on_severity': no default/else branch and no fall-through `next:`; if every condition evaluates false the run will stall. Either add a branch with `default: true` or set `next:` on the decision step

# Record-context trigger demo (cybersponse.action). Half of all
# real-world FSR playbooks fire this way — right-click an alert,
# pick "Execute". The playbook receives the record at vars.input.records[0]

# triggerOnSource, etc.) auto-filled.

collection: FSRPB Demo Alert Action
description: Right-click action on alerts — read severity, decide, stamp.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB Demo - Triage Alert
    description: Reads alert severity and tags the alert with a verdict.
    is_active: true
    steps:

rows.yaml:
# post_create trigger demo. Fires automatically when an alert is
# created where `type` matches a known suspicious-email value (uses
# the `when:` shorthand → fieldbasedtrigger). Single-step playbook
# stamps the new alert with a marker so the e2e runner can verify the
# trigger fired.

collection: FSRPB Demo Alert On Create
description: Auto-trigger when a Phishing alert is created.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB Demo - On Alert Create
    is_active: true
    steps:
      - name: On Alert Create
        type: start_on_create
        next: Stamp marker
        arguments:
          module: alerts
          when:
            logic: AND
            filters:
              # `name` is a plain string field; picklist-typed fields
              # like `type`/`severity` store IRIs, not display values,
              # so a literal-string `like` filter on them never matches.
              - {field: name, op: like, value: "%fsrpb_e2e_on_create%"}

      - name: Stamp marker
        type: update_record
        arguments:
          collection: "{{ vars.input.records[0]['@id'] }}"
          module: alerts
          resource:
            description: "fsrpb_e2e_on_create_marker"
          operation: Replace
          step_variables: []

warnings.message: UpdateRecord.arguments.operation='Replace' not seen in any of the corpus samples for this step type

rows.yaml:
# post_update trigger demo (cybersponse.post_update). Fires automatically
# whenever an alert is updated AND the `status` field changed. Single-step
# playbook stamps the alert with a marker so the e2e runner can verify
# the trigger fired.
#
# `type: start_on_update` + `when:` shorthand expands to FSR's canonical
# `fieldbasedtrigger` block with `operator: changed`.

collection: FSRPB Demo Alert On Status Change
description: Auto-trigger when an alert's status field changes.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB Demo - On Alert Status Change
    is_active: true
    steps:
      - name: On Alert Status Change
        type: start_on_update
        next: Stamp marker
        arguments:
          module: alerts
          when:
            logic: OR
            filters:
              - {field: status, op: changed}

      - name: Stamp marker
        type: update_record
        arguments:
          collection: "{{ vars.input.records[0]['@id'] }}"
          module: alerts                # → collectionType: /api/3/alerts
          resource:
            description: "fsrpb_e2e_on_update_marker"
          operation: Replace
          step_variables: []

warnings.message: UpdateRecord.arguments.operation='Replace' not seen in any of the corpus samples for this step type

rows.yaml:
# CodeSnippet (Python inline) demo. Runs a small Python block through
# FSR's `code-snippet` connector. Compiler resolves the connector config
# UUID via connector_configs.resolve_config_id (live + cached).
#
# Authoring shape: just `code:` (multiline string). Optional `config:`
# selects a non-default connector configuration by friendly name.

collection: FSRPB Demo Code Snippet
description: Run a Python snippet, capture output, branch on result.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB Demo - Code Snippet
    is_active: true
    parameters: [first_name]
    steps:
      - name: trigger
        type: start
        next: Build greeting

      - name: Build greeting
        type: code_snippet
        next: Stamp greeting
        arguments:
          code: |
            name = "{{ vars.input.params.first_name }}"
            greeting = f"hello {name}"
            print(greeting)

      - name: Stamp greeting
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          greeting_text: "{{ vars.steps.Build_greeting.output }}"

# Delay step demo. Single-step body that pauses for 2 seconds, then a
# set_variable stamps a marker so we can verify the step actually ran.
# Compiler expands `arguments: {seconds: 2}` to the canonical FSR
# TimeBased rule shape (resume_playbook channel + crudhub event source).

collection: FSRPB Demo Delay
description: Verify the delay step runs to completion.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB Demo - Delay
    is_active: true
    steps:
      - name: trigger
        type: start
        next: Wait 2s

      - name: Wait 2s
        type: delay
        next: Stamp done
        arguments:
          seconds: 2

      - name: Stamp done
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          marker: "delay_passed"

# for_each demo. A Set Variables step builds a static list of three
# alert payloads; the next step loops over them with `for_each`,
# creating one alert per element. Each iteration's element is bound
# as `{{ vars.item }}` (object fields under `vars.item.<field>`).
#
# Note: this uses non-bulk Create Record so on-create alert playbook
# triggers fire normally — the ingestion-recipe pattern. Threat-feed
# recipes use IngestBulkFeed with for_each.__bulk=true instead.

collection: FSRPB Demo For Each
description: Loops a create_record over a list with for_each / vars.item.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB For Each Create
    is_active: true
    steps:
      - name: trigger
        type: start
        next: Build alerts

      - name: Build alerts
        type: set_variable
        next: Create alert per item
        vars:
          alerts_to_create:
            - name: "fsrpb_for_each marker A"
              severity: High
              description: "first looped alert"
            - name: "fsrpb_for_each marker B"
              severity: Medium
              description: "second looped alert"
            - name: "fsrpb_for_each marker C"
              severity: Low
              description: "third looped alert"

      - name: Create alert per item
        type: create_record
        for_each:
          item: "{{ vars.steps.Build_alerts.alerts_to_create }}"
          parallel: false
          condition: ""
        arguments:
          module: alerts
          operation: Replace
          resource:
            name: "{{ vars.item.name }}"
            severity: "{{ vars.item.severity }}"
            description: "{{ vars.item.description }}"

warnings.message: InsertData.arguments.step_variables is missing — every corpus sample for this step type sets this key

rows.yaml:
# Manual-input (human-in-the-loop) demo. Pauses with two options;
# whichever the user clicks routes to a different next step. The e2e
# runner picks the option programmatically via `resume_input:` in the
# .test.yaml so the demo can run unattended.

collection: FSRPB Demo Manual Input
description: Pause for an approve/reject decision, then branch.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB Demo - Manual Input
    is_active: true
    steps:
      - name: trigger
        type: start
        next: Approve action

      - name: Approve action
        type: manual_input
        options:
          - display: approve
            primary: true
            next: Stamp approved
          - display: reject
            next: Stamp rejected
        arguments:
          title: "Approve the e2e action?"
          description: "Click **approve** to continue, **reject** to abort."

      - name: Stamp approved
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          verdict: approved

      - name: Stamp rejected
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          verdict: rejected

# create_record demo. The playbook itself creates an alert (rather than
# the test sidecar's setup) — exercises the InsertData step type.
#
# Cleanup: the playbook tags the new alert's `description` with a known
# fsrpb_e2e_create marker; the .test.yaml's cleanup_query then deletes
# any alert with that marker so repeat runs don't accumulate.

collection: FSRPB Demo Record Create
description: Single-step playbook that creates an alert.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB Create Alert
    is_active: true
    parameters: [alert_name]
    steps:
      - name: trigger
        type: start
        next: Create alert

      - name: Create alert
        type: create_record
        arguments:
          module: alerts                # → collection: /api/3/alerts
          operation: Replace
          resource:
            name: "{{ vars.input.params.alert_name }}"
            severity: /api/3/picklists/40187287-89fc-4e9c-b717-e9443d57eedb   # High
            description: "fsrpb_e2e_create marker"
          step_variables: []

# Find + Update record CRUD demo. Covers ~38% of corpus (FindRecords +
# UpdateRecord). Setup inserts a tagged test alert; this playbook
# finds it by uuid, then updates its description field.
#
# FSR canonical shapes (verified live 2026-05-03):
#   find_record arguments:
#     module:    'alerts'    — the module name. Suffix `?$limit=N`
#                              optional but the API Platform respects it.
#     query:                  — query DSL
#       logic:    'AND' | 'OR'
#       filters:  [{type:'primitive', field, value, operator, _operator}]
#       __selectFields: list of fields to fetch (optional)
#     step_variables: []
#
#   update_record arguments:
#     collection:     IRI of the record to update (Jinja from find_record output)
#     collectionType: module IRI (e.g. /api/3/alerts)
#     resource:       {field: value, ...} fields to set
#     operation:      'Replace' | 'Append'
#     step_variables: []

collection: FSRPB Demo Record CRUD
description: Find a tagged alert and update it.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: FSRPB Find And Update Alert
    is_active: true
    parameters: [target_uuid]
    steps:
      - name: trigger
        type: start
        next: Find target alert

      - name: Find target alert
        type: find_record
        next: Stamp description
        arguments:
          module: alerts
          query:
            logic: AND
            filters:
              - type: primitive
                field: uuid
                value: "{{ vars.input.params.target_uuid }}"
                operator: eq
                _operator: eq
            __selectFields: [uuid, name, description]
          step_variables: []

      - name: Stamp description
        type: update_record
        arguments:
          collection: "{{ vars.steps.Find_target_alert[0]['@id'] }}"
          collectionType: /api/3/alerts
          resource:
            description: "fsrpb e2e — stamped at {{ vars.task_id }}"
          operation: Replace
          step_variables: []

warnings.message: UpdateRecord.arguments.operation='Replace' not seen in any of the corpus samples for this step type

rows.yaml:
# Demo A backbone: real VirusTotal IP-reputation lookup, branched on the
# real `last_analysis_stats.malicious` count, no hardcoding. The runner
# passes an IP via parameters; the playbook calls VT live and stamps a
# verdict.
#
# This is the smallest playbook that actually demonstrates the full
# SOAR pattern (input → enrichment → decision → action) end-to-end on
# a real connector against a real API.

collection: FSRPB Demo VirusTotal
description: Look up an IP on VirusTotal and stamp a verdict by malicious count.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: VT IP Reputation
    description: Real VT lookup → branch on last_analysis_stats.malicious.
    is_active: true
    parameters: [ip]
    steps:
      - name: start
        type: start
        next: Query VirusTotal

      - name: Query VirusTotal
        type: connector
        next: Extract malicious count
        arguments:
          connector: virustotal
          operation: query_ip
          config: ""
          params:
            ip: "{{ vars.input.params.ip }}"

      - name: Extract malicious count
        type: set_variable
        next: Branch on verdict
        vars:
          malicious_count: "{{ vars.steps.Query_VirusTotal.data.attributes.last_analysis_stats.malicious }}"

      - name: Branch on verdict
        type: decision
        conditions:
          - display: malicious
            when: "{{ (vars.malicious_count | int) > 0 }}"
            next: Stamp malicious
          - display: clean
            when: "{{ (vars.malicious_count | int) == 0 }}"
            next: Stamp clean

      - name: Stamp malicious
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          verdict: "malicious"

      - name: Stamp clean
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          verdict: "clean"

warnings.message: decision 'branch_on_verdict': no default/else branch and no fall-through `next:`; if every condition evaluates false the run will stall. Either add a branch with `default: true` or set `next:` on the decision step

rows.yaml:
# manual_input — pause execution and ask a human a question.
# Common in approval flows: bot proposes an action, user confirms.
#
# Prompt body under `arguments:` — `title:` is the heading,
# `description:` is the markdown body, `inputs:` is an optional
# inputVariables list. Step-level `options:` is the button list; each
# entry has `display:` (label) and `next:` (target step). The choice
# IS the branch taken — there is no `vars.steps.<name>.input.choice`
# at runtime.

collection: Compiler Examples
description: Approval loop — ask a human, then act based on their response.
visible: true

playbooks:
  - name: Confirm Before Block
    description: Ask SOC analyst to approve a block, then act.
    steps:
      - name: start
        type: start
        next: Approve block

      - name: Approve block
        type: manual_input
        options:
          - display: block
            primary: true
            next: Pretend we blocked
          - display: skip
            next: Skip
        arguments:
          title: Block this IP?
          description: Click **block** to proceed, **skip** to abort.

      - name: Pretend we blocked
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          outcome: blocked

      - name: Skip
        type: set_variable
        vars:
          outcome: skipped
